**Feature request:** Add a uniform interface for accessing the minimum or maximum value of a given dtype.

Previously discussed [here](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/5032#issuecomment-1830838701), [here](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/5032#issuecomment-2307927804), and [here](https://github.com/google/jax/issues/18661#issuecomment-1829031914). Currently, doing this requires branching on the type of dtype (boolean, integer, or floating point) and then (for the latter two) calling either [iinfo](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.iinfo.html) or [finfo](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.finfo.html), respectively. It would be more ergonomic to have a single, uniform interface for accessing this information that is dtype-independent.

Here are the possible interfaces suggested so far:

```python3
import numpy as np
dt = np.int32 # example

# As an attribute of the dtype itself:
dt.min
dt.min_value
dt.info().min
dt.info.min

# As a function in numpy.dtypes:
np.dtypes.info(dt).min
np.dtypes.min(dt)
np.dtypes.min_value(dt)

# As a top-level function:
np.min_dtype_value(dt)
np.dtype_info(dt).min
np.dtype_info(dt).min_value
```

Personally, my current favorite is simply `dt.min`.

Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

The [`linprog`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.optimize.linprog.html) documentation contains the following description of the `x0` argument:

> Guess values of the decision variables, which will be refined by the optimization algorithm. <ins>This argument is currently used only by the ‘revised simplex’ method</ins>, and can only be used if x0 represents a basic feasible solution.

Currently, there is no way to pass an initial guess of the solution (this could be, for example, the solution to a similar problem) to [`linprog(method='highs')`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/optimize.linprog-highs.html) to speed up optimization.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

Extend the implementation of [`linprog(method='highs')`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/optimize.linprog-highs.html) to use the `x0` argument to [`linprog`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.optimize.linprog.html) as an initial guess.

Describe your issue.

[`scipy.special.logsumexp`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.special.logsumexp.html) raises a `ValueError` for a zero-size array (see below).

Mathematically, the correct answer for a zero-size array is $\log(\text{empty sum}) = \log(0) = -\infty$.

This error is caused by the [use](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/e2e39f691c048b8ad6dd5e7d4eb5a396a81f8dd3/scipy/special/_logsumexp.py#L105C13-L105C20) of [`np.amax`](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.amax.html) without specifying `initial=-inf`.

**Solution:** Specify `initial=-np.inf` in the call to `np.amax`. I can submit a PR for this.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
python3 -c "import scipy; print(scipy.special.logsumexp([]))"
```

    return ufunc.reduce(obj, axis, dtype, out, **passkwargs)

ValueError: zero-size array to reduction operation maximum which has no identity

Problem

Using [`axhline`](https://matplotlib.org/stable/api/_as_gen/matplotlib.pyplot.axline.html), one can easily plot an infinite line:

```python3
def plot_line(ax, a, b, c, **kwargs):
    """Plots the solution to a x + b y = c."""
    ax.axline((c / a, 0), (0, c / b), **kwargs)
```

It would be nice to have an easy way to plot an infinite [half space](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Half-space_(geometry)):

```python3
def plot_half_space(ax, a, b, c):
    """Plots the solution to a x + b y ≥ c."""
    ...
```

### Proposed solution

This could potentially be done by adding a `HalfSpace` patch to [`matplotlib.patches`](https://matplotlib.org/stable/api/patches_api.html).

Just as the
* sum of an empty array is the [identity element](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/identity+element) of addition, which is [zero](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/zero)
* prod of an empty array is the identity element of multiplication, which is [one](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/one)

It ought to be the case that the
* max of an empty array is the identity element of [max](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/join), which is the [least element](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/bottom) of its dtype
* min of an empty array is the identity element of [min](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/meet), which is [greatest element](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/top) of its dtype

Summary

As pointed out in https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/596#issuecomment-322996723 and https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/596#issuecomment-323651892, `pause` should not raise the window by default. There are many users who have trouble with that behavior:
- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/44278369/how-to-keep-matplotlib-python-window-in-background
- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/45729092/make-interactive-matplotlib-window-not-pop-to-front-on-each-update-windows-7/45734500

### Proposed fix

One way to remedy this would be to move away from the global rcParam approach and do something like the following:
1. Add an explicit `raise_window` function.
2. Add a `raise_window` argument for `plot` (`True` by default).
3. Add a `raise_window` argument for `pause` (`False` by default).

In general, it seems like a good idea to make semantically-distinct actions (redrawing, window raising, pausing for a specified time interval, etc.) orthogonal rather than mix them together and then have to untangle them via global parameters. This might also simplify the code for the various backends. Describe your issue.

The documentation for [scipy.special.betainc](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.special.betainc.html) should say "_regularized_ incomplete beta function" instead of "incomplete beta function" everywhere, especially up front. The latter almost universally refers to the unregularized version (see e.g. [here](https://mathworld.wolfram.com/IncompleteBetaFunction.html)). Not clarifying this up front can cause confusion and bugs (I had to chase down a bug caused by this.) See #1350 and #14744 for previous related issues.

Summary

As pointed out in https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/596#issuecomment-322996723 and https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/596#issuecomment-323651892, `pause` should not raise the window by default. There are many users who have trouble with that behavior:
- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/44278369/how-to-keep-matplotlib-python-window-in-background
- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/45729092/make-interactive-matplotlib-window-not-pop-to-front-on-each-update-windows-7/45734500

### Proposed fix

One way to remedy this would be to move away from the global rcParam approach and do something like the following:
1. Add an explicit `raise_window` function.
2. Add a `raise_window` argument for `plot` (`True` by default).
3. Add a `raise_window` argument for `pause` (`False` by default).

In general, it seems like a good idea to make semantically-distinct actions (redrawing, window raising, pausing for a specified time interval, etc.) orthogonal rather than mix them together and then have to untangle them via global parameters.
